draft.introduction: Isaiah 65:17–25 contains the promise of “new heavens and a new earth,” where former sorrows are forgotten and the wolf and the lamb feed together. This vision of cosmic renewal shapes both Jewish and Christian eschatology. From the Septuagint.

translation.text: For behold, I create new heavens and a new earth, and the former things shall not be remembered or come into mind. But be glad and rejoice forever in that which I create; for behold, I create Jerusalem to be a joy, and her people to be a gladness. I will rejoice in Jerusalem and be glad in my people; no more shall be heard in it the sound of weeping and the cry of distress.
translation.original_text: ἔσται γὰρ ὁ οὐρανὸς καινὸς καὶ ἡ γῆ καινή

translation.text: The wolf and the lamb shall graze together; the lion shall eat straw like the ox, and dust shall be the serpent’s food. They shall not hurt or destroy in all my holy mountain, says the Lord.

translator_notes.note: The phrase “new heavens and a new earth” (οὐρανὸς καινὸς καὶ γῆ καινή) is taken up directly in 2 Peter 3:13 and Revelation 21:1. The vision is not of escape from the material world but of its transformation. Creation is not abandoned but renewed.
